relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: After Joshua dies, Israel asks who will go first into the unfinished struggle.
Judah is named as the first tribe to rise.
Judah does not go alone but asks Simeon to share the campaign.
Bezek becomes the first field of victory and blood.
Adoni-bezek is found inside the battle he helped create.
His body is mutilated in retaliation.
He recognizes the violence he practiced returning upon him.
Jerusalem is struck and burned in one strand of the memory.
Judah turns from city to hill, southland, and lowland.
Hebron's Anakim names return beneath Judah's attack.
Debir, once Kiriath-sepher, becomes the next target.
Caleb offers Achsah as the reward for conquest.
Othniel captures the city and receives Achsah within the patriarchal bargain.
Achsah moves within the arrangement and presses for material provision.
She asks for springs because dry land cannot sustain life.
The Kenites, Moses' in-law people, settle with Judah in the south.
Judah and Simeon destroy Zephath, naming it Hormah.
Coastal cities are claimed in Judah's campaign memory.
The hill country yields, but iron chariots hold the valleys.
Hebron is given to Caleb, and Anak's sons are displaced.
Jebusites remain in Jerusalem with Benjamin.
Joseph's house rises toward Bethel with YHWH's presence.
Bethel is watched under its older name, Luz.
A local man is asked to reveal the city's entrance in exchange for covenant loyalty.
The city falls, but the informant and his clan are spared.
He builds another Luz among Hittites, carrying a city-name into survival elsewhere.
Manasseh's map is full of Canaanites determined to remain.
When Israel grows stronger, coexistence becomes forced labor.
Gezer remains Canaanite within Ephraim.
Zebulun also leaves Canaanites in place under labor.
Asher does not dispossess coastal and northern towns.
Asher lives among Canaanite inhabitants rather than replacing them.
Naphtali's remaining towns also become labor sources.
Dan is pressed upward by Amorite power.
Amorites remain determined until Joseph's heavy hand turns them into labor.
The chapter closes with an Amorite border still visible.

contemplative_note: Judges 1 refuses the clean ending Joshua seemed to offer. The land is mixed, contested, partially held, and ethically compromised. Achsah asks for water, Jebusites and Canaanites remain, and survival is too often turned into forced labor. This is not completion; it is unresolved inheritance.
